chunk:
CHUNK:021/021
“And we need to know who.” Gaba pulled out his phone and stepped out of the cabin, leaving me alone to peel back the towels and discover just how unreadable Paniula’s diary had become. Several of the pages were stuck together, and I didn’t dare peel them apart for fear of tearing them. But one of the pages was hanging out, and I tugged it free, only to realise it wasn’t a page from the diary, but something else that had been tucked inside. I turned around as Gaba burst back into the room, wearing a look I just knew could only mean trouble. “It’s him,” he said, clenching his fist around his phone. “It was August.” “Barry?” “Aap,” Gaba said. “He called the commissioner. He was the commissioner’s contact. We’ve been set up from the start. It was August’s idea to use Rachael. Can you believe that? He basically told me the play, and how to play it. He must have smuggled the diamonds onto the cruise ship, and then stepped off somewhere. He’s so bland he could have been anyone. But we’re not done…” “Gaba…” “This isn’t over, Petra. The commissioner is just as mad as I am. We’re going to get him. I have no idea how. But it’s a done deal. We’ll get him.” “Gaba, listen,” I said, as I plucked the extra page out of the diary. I waved it in front of him. “And what’s supposed to be?” “It’s what Rachael was going to use to negotiate a better deal with Sean. Or Barry.” I frowned and said, “I’m not sure. But whatever she wanted, he obviously wasn’t going to give it to her. And now…” “Now what?” Gaba said. “Now we’ve got he wants. You want to get him?” Gaba nodded. “Then this is how we do it.” “Right,” Gaba said. He reached for the MP5 and started putting it back together. “Gaba,” I said. “What?” “You’ve got a plane to catch.” He stopped what he was doing and then shook his head. “You mean we. Right?” “No,” I said with a nod at Paniula’s diary. “I have to catch up on my reading.” Gaba took a moment to take the hint, but I shooed him out of the cabin with the promise of delivering the page torn from the guestbook as soon as it was dry. But until then, I was going to try my very best to enjoy a Greenland cruise in the way it was supposed to be done. Slowly. Under the midnight sun. The End 
 Your words make all the difference Honest reviews of my books help other readers find them. As an independent author I don’t have the backing of a publisher or a team of publicists, but I do have one thing going for me, and that’s a group of engaged readers. If you enjoyed this book I would be very grateful if you could spend just five minutes leaving a review (as short as you like) on the book’s Amazon page. You can find the page easily by clicking on the link below. The Invisible Thread Your words make all the difference. Your support allows me to write more words, more books, and more adventures. Thank you. 
 Get more Stories I have written a lot of stories set in Scandinavia and the Arctic. Many of them are interconnected with characters making guest appearances across the different series. If you’d like to try more of my stories and want the latest news and releases, please consider following my blog. You can also download a free copy of Piteraq. It’s the prequel to my adrenaline-fueled Greenland Trilogy. You can get your FREE book by clicking here. I look forward to telling you more about Greenland and the Arctic regions I have fallen in love with. Chris 
 About the Author Christoffer Petersen is the author’s pen name. He lives in Denmark. Chris started writing stories about Greenland while teaching in Qaanaaq, the largest village in the very north of Greenland – the population peaked at 600 during the two years he lived there. Chris spent a total of seven years in Greenland, teaching in remote communities and at the Police Academy in the capital of Nuuk. You can find Chris in Denmark or online here: Christoffer Petersen or on Facebook. For exclusive content check out Christoffer Petersen’s Patreon page. 
 This is a work of fiction. Similarities to real people, places, or events, are entirely coincidental. THE INVISIBLE THREAD First edition. February 6, 2024. Copyright © 2024 Christoffer Petersen. Written by Christoffer Petersen. Cover by Chris Paton 
"
}
